title: SEGMENT 7 — The molecular crime scene — how the SVA insertion created a cryptic splice site

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — The molecular crime scene — how the SVA insertion created a cryptic splice site
So we have an SVA — about two thousand bases of foreign sequence — sitting inside intron 6 of MFSD8 on Mila's second chromosome.
Now I want to walk you through, in slow motion, what that insertion actually does to the gene.
Because to understand the drug, you have to understand the broken machinery the drug is trying to compensate for.
And the broken machinery is, in this case, a problem with RNA splicing.
Quick refresher, in case it has been a while since your intro biology class.
When a gene is transcribed, the initial RNA product — called the pre-mRNA — contains both the exons and the introns of the gene, in their original order.

Before that RNA can be translated into protein, the introns have to be cut out, and the exons have to be joined together end to end, to produce the mature messenger RNA that the ribosome will actually read.
This cut-and-paste operation is called splicing, and it is performed by a large molecular machine in the nucleus called the spliceosome.
The spliceosome recognizes the boundaries between exons and introns by reading short sequence motifs in the pre-mRNA.
The end of each exon, going into an intron, is marked by what we call a splice donor site.
The start of each exon, coming out of an intron, is marked by a splice acceptor site.
These sites are short — only a few bases of recognizable motif — and they are recognized by complementary sequences in the spliceosome's RNA components.
When the spliceosome reads a donor site, it knows where one cut has to be made.
When it reads an acceptor site, it knows where the next cut has to be made and where the next exon begins.
It makes the cuts, throws the intron away, and welds the two exons together.
This is going on, in your cells, thousands of times per second, across every gene that's being expressed.
In a healthy MFSD8 gene, the splicing of intron 6 works the normal way.
The spliceosome reads the donor site at the end of exon 6, reads the acceptor site at the start of exon 7, cuts both ends, throws out intron 6, and welds exon 6 directly to exon 7.
The mature mRNA contains the full coding sequence, and the resulting MFSD8 protein is functional.
Now, what happens when an SVA is jammed into intron 6?
Most of the time, intronic insertions are still tolerated by the spliceosome.
The intron gets longer, but it still gets spliced out, because the donor at the end of exon 6 and the acceptor at the start of exon 7 are still where they're supposed to be.
The spliceosome still finds them and still does its job.
A longer intron is, mostly, no big deal.
But sometimes — and this is exactly what happened in Mila's gene — the inserted SVA contains, within its own sequence, a short motif that looks, to the spliceosome, like a splice acceptor.
A fake acceptor.
A motif that the spliceosome's RNA components can base-pair with, and that the spliceosome therefore reads as a legitimate place to make a cut.
We call these fake sites cryptic splice sites.
They exist all over the genome — in introns, in intergenic regions, even within exons — but they normally aren't used because the spliceosome preferentially uses the real, strong sites.

When a cryptic site is introduced by a mutation in a position where the spliceosome can now reach it, however, the cryptic site can get used in preference to the real site, and the splicing pattern gets corrupted.
Here is the analogy I want you to use.
A cryptic splice acceptor is a fake doorway.
Imagine a corridor in a building, with a door at one end labeled "Exit.
" Everyone uses that door, because it's where the exit is.
Now someone installs a fake door, made of paint and a doorknob, partway down the corridor, also labeled "Exit.
" People hurrying through the corridor may now hit the fake door first, try to open it, get confused, and end up in the wrong place.
The legitimate exit at the end of the corridor is still there, but the fake one earlier in the corridor is now intercepting traffic that was supposed to reach the legitimate one.
That is exactly what happens, molecularly, when an SVA introduces a cryptic splice acceptor into an intron.
In Mila's case, the relevant cryptic acceptor sat about 119 base pairs upstream of the real exon 7 splice acceptor.
So the spliceosome, working its way through intron 6, reaches the cryptic acceptor first — and uses it.
The cut happens at the wrong place.
The exon being added to the growing mRNA is not exon 7.
It is the chunk of intronic sequence between the cryptic acceptor and the real exon 7, which we'll call "i6.
SA-derived" — meaning, derived from the intron-6-cryptic-acceptor exonization.
And here is the lethal consequence.
That mis-spliced exon, that piece of intronic sequence that has been inappropriately included in the mRNA, contains within it a premature stop codon.
A sequence — TAA or TAG or TGA, the three nucleotide triplets that signal "stop translation here" — appears in the wrong reading frame, much earlier than it should.
So when the ribosome translates this corrupted mRNA into protein, it gets a short way in, hits the premature stop, and falls off.
No full-length MFSD8 protein is made from this allele.
The cell tries — the gene is transcribed, the broken splicing happens, the truncated protein attempts to be produced — and the result is essentially nothing.
A truncated fragment of MFSD8 that does not function.

So the SVA, sitting in intron 6, has effectively destroyed Mila's second copy of MFSD8 — not by deleting the gene, not by mutating the protein-coding sequence, but by inserting a cryptic splice site that hijacks the splicing machinery and produces a truncated, non-functional protein.
The exonic sequence of MFSD8 on that chromosome is intact.
Every exon, sequenced individually, looks normal.
The damage is purely at the level of how the gene is processed.
That is why the panel didn't see anything wrong — because the panel was looking at the exons, and the exons are fine.
The crime took place not in the exons themselves but in the spliceosome's interpretation of them.
I want to dwell on this for one more moment, because the molecular shape of this problem is what sets up the entire treatment strategy.
The problem here is not that Mila is missing the MFSD8 gene.
She has it.
The problem is not that the protein-coding sequence has been corrupted.
It hasn't.
The problem is that the splicing of one specific intron is going wrong, because of one specific cryptic splice site that the SVA has introduced, that the spliceosome is now using when it shouldn't.

If you could somehow prevent the spliceosome from using that cryptic site — if you could put something over the fake doorway so that the spliceosome's reading machinery skipped over it and went on to find the real exon 7 acceptor — then the splicing would proceed normally, exon 6 would be joined directly to exon 7, and the resulting mRNA would encode a full-length, functional MFSD8 protein.

From this one allele, you would get half-normal levels of working transporter, which combined with whatever the c.1102G>C allele produces should be more than enough to substantially rescue the cell's lysosomal function.
That is the therapeutic concept.
And the molecule that can do this masking — the sticky note that can cover the fake doorway — is an antisense oligonucleotide.
That is the topic of Segment 8.
